item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3126 is an African-American woman, sold off as property by a white woman to a rich (presumably white from context, still fucked up either way) famous person.

Do you see the issue here?

Oh. Wow, yeah. That flew right over my head.

An SCP with such subtle horror deserves better than my downvote. Upgrading to neutral.

If you were writing this as a wider commentary on its own about how deeply-rooted the issue of racism is even in art communities and among the elite, that such a power imbalance would let this sort of thing happen and how it does happen in the world but specifically (in this case) the Western world, then this wouldn't have left such a bad taste in my mouth.

Do you need a full diatribe regarding the horrors of war in every work featuring a veteran with implied PTSD? What about the socio-economic causes of poverty and crime in superhero comics?

Racism is horrible, but it's no different from any other horrible thing. Treating it as taboo is counterproductive.

I can not conceive of anyone who isn't white being okay with selling someone into slavery

No offense, but that's at least as racist as assuming a rapist is black.

If you want to add the qualifier "in 1900s America", I can add the qualifier "in downtown Chicago". It's still a form of racial profiling at best.

There's also the fact that you've written in an explicit lesbian couple for this article, a real rarity on the entire site, and it displays a serious power imbalance between a white woman and an African-American woman, as she continues to have her power stripped away from her culminating in being sold off for profit to others. It gives this sort of weird, mid-1900s vibe to the whole thing with how these lesbians are being portrayed, and it bothers me.

In the 2010's, though, the general understanding is that homosexual relationships are basically the same as heterosexual ones. It thus follows that they have the same potential for becoming abusive.

Again, a taboo is not in any way helpful. True equality means homosexuals are subject to the same spectrum of positive and negative depictions as heterosexuals.

I will say though, that your issues with the AWCY? portrayal are entirely valid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're the original author: my understanding is that you can totally rewrite or even delete this at your leisure.

I advise focusing your efforts on improving the science and reducing cringe, though.

As others pointed out, 1 Tesla is huge. Even a few milli-Tesla is enough to move paperclips. For lesser magnetic fields, I'd be more worried about the current flowing through your average power cable than anything a PC produces (unless you're physically touching the hard drive).

The link to SCP-1057 looks like you're trying to ride on another article's popularity — if you're really set on forcing a reference to another SCP in, at least choose something that could use more exposure. Also, the way you had her recognize the "S.C.P." initials basically put a spotlight on an ugly plothole.

I wouldn't worry about "fixing" the "racial issues". For an SCP, having an accidental extra layer of disturbing implications is actually a good thing. Leaving the deeper social 'power imbalance' unexplored is fine, since it's clear you're not depicting this as a normal or acceptable state of affairs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You realize the irony of saying 'it's about humans, not colors' to someone of mixed-race, right?

Anyways, I was specifically trying to evoke early-mid 1800s slave auctions in America with that part, and Bellamy was always a red-haired light-skinned woman of French descent in my mind. More to the point, I agree with her that, given how the oppression of non-white people by white people is ingrained in Western history and culture, assuming Bellamy's white is what most people would do. That's why I didn't bother to specify. So there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Part of what drove me to attempt an AWCY?-related SCP was to, in fact, do something different. There's a short story in the AWCY? tag where The Critic (well, A Critic, knowing the fragmented nature of AWCY) goes on a rant about how so much of AWCY?'s output is focused around inflicting suffering. More about harming people than actually being artful. That's why the actual art piece explores its concept (i.e. expanding the definition of what can be called a statue) in a way that isn't inherently harmful—the problem only arises because they didn't understand the tools they were using. Additionally, Gloria is the victim of actions perpetrated by a member of AWCY?, yes, but the third interview and a bit of the second imply that she's also a believer in the principles under AWCY? herself (if an estranged one for obvious reasons). She therefore serves as a counterpoint to Bellamy's more questionable actions. Horrible things are done, but there is also a legitimate desire to explore artistic ideas, and I feel that's enough to separate it from the usual 'AWCY? are just trolls hiding behind art' stereotypes.

I will admit that Bellamy ended up being a bit more of an asshole than I intended. Earlier versions had Bellamy either get talked into the sale, or the sale happening behind her back, or her disappearing for ambiguous reasons without leaving instructions on how to unstatue Gloria. And given the genders and orientations in play, that led to some implications that I didn't intend. I also could have fleshed out the second and third interviews a bit, and made some of the more redeeming elements more explicit but I was already nervous about the interviews having too much exposition and I didn't want to overdo it. That said, while the auction was a late-game addition and I didn't initially realize the racial implications, I intentionally left it in after the realization. One of the themes running through the article is objectification, with Gloria literally turned into an object for the sake of art and eventually ending up with the SCP, which objectifies everything it contains by default (and that, in turn, flows into the general objectification of black Americans by white Americans as a whole and white artists and entertainers in particular, but I digress). I, as a Black man in America, thought that the connection to American slavery auctions dovetailed rather nicely.

I could always go back in and give Bellamy a bit more credit in the second interview, I guess. Not sure what the etiquette is for rewriting a work after it's already been posted, as opposed to editing out mistakes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nevermind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I addressed this above. My post was literally in the context of 1990s America. Like, that was the context of the post alone but I even elaborated it later on in the thread. I know this already, and I took it into consideration when making my replies. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and I can not conceive of anyone who isn't white being okay with selling someone into slavery,

I am not really sure what this means.

It is pretty clear from the real world that people who are non-white are fine with selling people into slavery. Most of the slavery in the modern world is in South Asia and North Africa, and even going back to the historical American slave trade, the original sellers of African slaves were always other black Africans or Arabs.

Nowadays I would expect that most Europeans and Americans find slavery to be morally abhorrent, and it certainly isn't legal or tolerated in the EU or the Americas. It is illegal but tolerated in many places in the developing world, and is legal and even encouraged in some places (for example ISIS practices slavery as a policy in the territory they control).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
or do bastard people become anartists

Probably this. I mean, it takes some kind of inherent bastardy to knowingly affect people with more than a temporary, short-lived anomalous effect (i.e., say viewing the art causes synesthesia for two hours) as part of an "artistic statement".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It takes a very strong assumption of bad faith on Deci's part to assume she meant that she somehow meant this.

I am confident that this was not her conscious intention. I stand by my point, however, in the hopes that it encourages her to consider the implications of a statement made with a similar level of extrapolation. Those who fight monsters and all that.

On the second part, that's only for the pure definition of Chattel Slavery. Exploitation takes many forms: at the same time that slavery was (allegedly) being ended for those legally considered property, there were immigrant workers dying by the thousands in mines, factory towns, and on railway projects while being repeatedly told at gunpoint that they had no right to question their employers or stop working in protest. It's a crappy time period, and simplifying it to simple black vs. white is learning entirely the wrong lesson. It's about humans, not colors. Or arbitrarily assigned social categories for that matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will say to avoid circles ad infinitum (and also because I have appointments to get to today <.<):

I am merely concerned that, by citing this as a reason to downvote the article, other authors would simply avoid the subject entirely instead of bothering with the possibility of being intensive.

I'd concede that there's perhaps a small chance that this would happen were it to be my sole reason. I made sure to specify it's not. Specific racism WRT this subject is not something I'm qualified to speak on personally (as I'm mixed Brazilian), but I think there's a good amount of people who would genuinely appreciate someone trying earnestly and in good faith while fucking up than someone who just doesn't care to try.

And again; I don't think the author did this intentionally, nor are they to really blame. I think it's easy to write something like this and overlook what the implications of the writing might be. And my intention here isn't to shame the author here; merely point out what I see and interpret and read, so that hopefully people understand where I'm coming from and how to improve on such things in the future. I take critique seriously; it's one of my passions whenever I do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it assumes that "white people" exist as a category

They do. White is a social category. That there is no objective quality that confers whiteness is beside the point (and, y'know, inherent in social categories).

subhumans who are inherently okay with slavery.

It takes a very strong assumption of bad faith on Deci's part to assume she meant that she somehow meant this. Without getting bogged down in whatabout-ery, slavery as a modern institution has been perpetrated primarily by countries where the ruling class either was or was descended from Western Europeans, and was perpetrated against non-whites, particularly Africans.

This reads mostly like pedantic hair-splitting in the interest of some weird #notallwhitepeople point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Plus, there's no actual correlation between giving long, thoughtful critique and people seeing this critique and deciding to not be diverse in their writing. There's not enough data to back up your assertion.

Expressed myself incorrectly there. One is, indeed, free to examine a piece as thoroughly as one wants. I am merely concerned that, by citing this as a reason to downvote the article, other authors would simply avoid the subject entirely instead of bothering with the possibility of being intensive.

I don't think there's conciliation to be found on this point. It stems from the fact you see the article as flawed in its ethos, and I don't concur.

Again, I agree with all points raised about AWCY?. It's a complex GOI, and they are so much more than a silly catch-phrase.

she was a trans woman who got brutally murdered for her art, I'd get comments from people who know that trans women get murdered brutally as hate crimes.

This is probably where our line of thinking differs the most. I did understand immediately what you meant in the first comment: If you don't understand physics, don't throw it in the middle of your article - this is true for any subject. It's just that, in 3126's case, I see nothing remotely comparable to your example.

There are two characters, one of which is a shithead and they happen to be from different ethnic groups. Looking further than that frankly seems like a stretch to me, which is probably another case of incompatibility between our interpretation of the piece.

But in 1990s America? I have to examine that in the context of the story. This isn't Django Unchained.

Quite fair. Still, as the article you linked very thoroughly documents, the scenario described is far from impossible.

I think you are completely justified in your vote - the article, again, does not depict AWCY? well and it seems to indeed offend you. I, personally, however, can't fault the author for interlacing what could be seen as a slavery allegory in the middle of a simple story about anomalies and abusive relationships, if you squint hard enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There seems to be this fairly recent trend of characterizing AWCY? as evil art bastards that do evil things for art

This isn't a new trend, though. AWCY has always been a group of art-terrorists doing terrible things that they pretentiously and ignorantly call art.

And the reason why I suspect all these characters who are not our skip are white is because that is the typical "default" for characters when their ethnicity and race is not specified, and I can not conceive of anyone who isn't white being okay with selling someone into slavery

I find this statement concerning on several levels. Firstly, it assumes that "white people" exist as a category1 and that they are subhumans who are inherently okay with slavery. I'll also point out that I cannot find a place where it specifies the girlfriend's race. You are assuming she is white because being white makes her capable of being a bad person, and that a non-white person would be incapable of the same act. There is a word for this. Several, actually.

Secondly, it ignores the massive global problem of human trafficking and the disturbing fetishization of pimping in numerous subcultures, and the concept of toxic relationships- all of which are problems that surpass melanin production rates and bone structure.

Footnotes
1. This is a relatively modern myth primarily invented and perpetuated by neo-nazis. "White" is not a culture, a genetic lineage, or a regional anatomical trait set. It is a trait developed independently by numerous branches of human migration in places where winter sucks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reading this much between the lines, in my opinion, could lead people to actually AVOID being more diverse when writing.

If one is going to create art for others to enjoy and take in, it's only fair that I should read as much as I want into the article as I can in order to critique it and understand it. Especially for an AWCY? article. I feel that AWCY? deserve the most in terms of actual in-depth art critique and analysis, since they are a heavily based in actually creating art, giving out a message with their art, and make their art loud and publicly enough to warrant people looking at them and absorbing their message. It's only fair that I take all AWCY? articles the same way. This is why they are, hands-down, my favorite group on the entire site. It'd be an injustice to go light here just because I'm "reading this much between the lines". Plus, there's no actual correlation between giving long, thoughtful critique and people seeing this critique and deciding to not be diverse in their writing. There's not enough data to back up your assertion.

it seems purely incidental - not only does it seem like the author had no interest going in such a direction

This is why it's bad. If I write an article about an art piece, and throw in some satellite elements that are incredibly glaring, I've distracted from my piece and drawn attention to it. If I write a piece about an artist who made an anomalous painting on the statement of money, and oh, she was a trans woman who got brutally murdered for her art, I'd get comments from people who know that trans women get murdered brutally as hate crimes.

Yes, I can sympathize with 3126 in the sense that she was stuck in a shitty relationship and eventually became the product of art assholes who exist purely to be assholes to other people (my major problem with the characterization of AWCY? here; see my post above). Yes, she was tricked by a selfish lover. But it's also tinted in such a way with everything else surrounding it that I feel justified in calling it out. It's not even the specific problem of someone being turned into a statue and then sold to someone else that specifically bothers me. It was the way it was handled here that bothers me.

Everybody can be an asshole.

Yes, that is true. This is true in that everyone is an asshole. It is also true in the sense that any ethnic group or race can and will sell others in slavery. This is a real interesting summary of that to read, and various citations lead to some facts I honestly didn't know about before. But in 1990s America? I have to examine that in the context of the story. This isn't Django Unchained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree wholeheartedly with the opinion on this not being a good AWCY? article. It's a neat skip, but doesn't use the GOI association in a way I particularly enjoy for pretty much all reasons stated.

As I a reader, however, I'll be brief and say the commentary on the characterization of Gloria and Bellamy feels unwarranted. While the story, unfortunately, does depicts an incredibly distasteful situation on the subject of race, it seems purely incidental - not only does it seem like the author had no interest going in such a direction but, also, the article is written in such a way people are supposed to sympathize with 3126 which, stuck into a shitty relationship, was a victim of a massive AWCY?-obsessed jerk.

This is a story about how a good person was tricked by her selfish lover. Reading this much between the lines, in my opinion, could lead people to actually AVOID being more diverse when writing. TL;DR:

I can not conceive of anyone who isn't white being okay with selling someone into slavery

This line pretty much sums it up in terms of what I disagree with. Everybody can be an asshole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There seems to be this fairly recent trend of characterizing AWCY? as evil art bastards that do evil things for art and to make statements with art. If that's where the site wants to take this then sure, but I think it's distressing considering these are the same guys who were really well-portrayed as… well, such a disparate group will never have one single unifying characteristic, but I thought for a time they were pretentious, in over their heads, but well-meaning ultimately. That's what drew me to them and made me adore them. Even Yoric's original tale that spawned them and the similar stuff created in that vein had a sense of do-gooder attitude even if they engaged in artistic terrorism (so to speak). This is… probably one of the least AWCY?-AWCY? articles I've seen in such a long time, probably since SCP-1800, a skip I loathe oh so much.

This doesn't quite reach those levels but I still am not a fan. I've been trying to think of what bothered me so much about this since I read it last night and here's what I got. I'm putting on my SJW hat for this.

SCP-3126 is an African-American woman, sold off as property by a white woman to a rich (presumably white from context, still fucked up either way) famous person.

Do you see the issue here?

If you were writing this as a wider commentary on its own about how deeply-rooted the issue of racism is even in art communities and among the elite, that such a power imbalance would let this sort of thing happen and how it does happen in the world but specifically (in this case) the Western world, then this wouldn't have left such a bad taste in my mouth. But the most you ever do with this is hint at the idea that maybe this is a theme that you have left unexplored, and this is assuming you were even conscious of what the problematic nature of this whole thing. If this was your overall objective, you failed because the best-case scenario is that you only ever vaguely allude to it. The worst-case scenario being that you didn't even realize you had these themes in the writing. But judging from your comments here and everyone else's, I think the latter is extremely likely.

There's also the fact that you've written in an explicit lesbian couple for this article, a real rarity on the entire site, and it displays a serious power imbalance between a white woman and an African-American woman, as she continues to have her power stripped away from her culminating in being sold off for profit to others. It gives this sort of weird, mid-1900s vibe to the whole thing with how these lesbians are being portrayed, and it bothers me.

And the reason why I suspect all these characters who are not our skip are white is because that is the typical "default" for characters when their ethnicity and race is not specified, and I can not conceive of anyone who isn't white being okay with selling someone into slavery, and if you did intend for this to happen to examine the ramifications and impact of it, and get across a meta-artistic message, you don't show this in your article. The most your main character, 3126, says about her old girlfriend in terms of how strong her emotion gets is that she just "wanted to be cool" (this style of referencing AWCY? got real old back in 2012, let alone 2017), and that "everything had to be a statement".

And what is AWCY?'s statement here? Because I read this article through so many times just to write this comment and try and word it as best as I can, and the closest statement that I can find is "art people are bastards", because I can't see any other reason provided by you in the article for why someone from this group would sell her girlfriend into slavery. I can't stress this enough; you don't even try and explore that specific thing in here. It just feels so… very not-AWCY?, and I'd have issues with this even if it weren't AWCY?, but specifically invoking them here makes this so much weaker than it is. It actively makes your message overall worse. Quoting someone from above:

Does being an anartist turn you into a bastard person, or do bastard people become anartists?

There's better ways to do that than invoke AWCY?'s name again for a tired article about them (which reminds me a lot about the heavy backlash they got when they got popular), and there's certainly more impactful and more stronger ways to do so than "African-American woman gets turned into art and sold into slavery". Because it would take extremely strong, smart, and thoughtful writing to turn that into a working article, though even trying for that would have been more respectful in my eyes than the product here.

Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not a time limit. Remember that she mentioned a yacht, and that the transformation's undone by high-salinity water, like seawater.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. Edit's been made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding the metric(imperial) thing. There's a couple series I's that get away with it because they're old, but modern articles are supposed to be metric only.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The confusion on the strength is understandable, though- the falloff on magnetic field strength is pretty steep: a refrigerator magnet is 5 mT, a neodynium magnet is 1.25 T, 16 T can levitate a frog, Sandia National Laboritory's Z-machine1 has a typical working load between ~10-100 T. They've gotten it up to a 1 kT pulse at least once, but they had to fix it afterward.

Footnotes
1. which is one of sexiest machines on Earth and basically the closest thing humanity has built to the Battleship Yamamoto's wave motion gun

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interview seems to imply that the effect lasts a maximum of 15 years, but I don't see that in the description.

It's so unsettling to think your loved one would just turn you into a statue and sell you. I love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The highlight of this article is the otherwise-rote biochemically-weird human skip being "aware" of the Foundation, and the off-kilter impressions of what she thought they did. It's a world-builder rather than an exhibit, and welcome as such because it doesn't try too hard in that vein.

All that, combined with being an AWCY piece that doesn't elicit a giant eyeroll, makes it an easy upvote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One, I know the effect is kind of subpar compared to other SCPs. I was going for something along the lines of SCP-1931, where it's less about the item itself and more about its place in its respective GoI. Herman Fuller's Circus for 1931 and AWCY for this one. This does mean that it's more reliant on its interviews than most SCPs, and I'm sorry it didn't click for you.

Two, if we're talking about superhero comparisons, forget Colossus, the Substitute Heroes' Stone Boy would outrank her.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So what if Colossus was a black woman with a considerably less-than-useful mutation.

Snark aside, this is decent, as X-Man-style humanoids go, but while the reason she was created was kind of interesting, overall it didn't do anything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does being an anartist turn you into a bastard person, or do bastard people become anartists?

This unfortunate woman… very well presented. I was simultaneously fascinated and horrified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also 1 Tesla is pretty high and you would need a supermagnet to reach those levels.

Uh, no. 1 Tesla is (slightly) weaker than most speaker coils.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's literally been years since an "Are we cool yet?" stinger got me. I didn't think it would ever happen again but you did it. I love the portrayal of the Foundation fifteen years in her past echoing the real history of how the Foundation appeared in an earlier period of SCP writing. The interviews sucked me in and felt powerful even in their brevity. Enthusiastic upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Been coming across a lot of are we cool yet scips of late. Is it a sign???

Also 1 Tesla is pretty high and you would need a supermagnet to reach those levels. So come.puters are way below that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So are we cool yet?

Great job tying your skip to a great GOI
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure if we should do the "metric (imperial)" format. I'm leaning against it, though i'd like a second opinion on that.

I like it otherwise, however, referring to but not straight mentioning the obvious affiliation is great, and I've always loved the idea of someone catching on to all the front companies. 👍
